text: This is a robotic ETEP ventral hernia repair complicated by posterior fascial dehiscence and a strategy for management. We have nothing to disclose. This is a fifty-one year old male presenting with a painful recurrent ventral hernia. Past medical history includes chronic constipation and obstructive sleep apnea.

Past surgical history includes a laparoscopic eye palm complicated by mesh infection, followed by laparoscopic removal of mesh, followed by hernia recurrence. Preoperative CT scan obtained reveals a significant diastasis ranging from five to six centimeters. The rectus muscles were robust, measuring about six centimeters on either side. There appeared to be a few small midline fascial defects, with the largest measuring three centimeters.

Based on these measurements, we felt the patient was a good candidate for endoscopic totally extraperitoneal access, or ETEP, with robotic retromuscular abdominal wall hernia repair using a lateral approach. We felt that the wide rectus would allow good working space and also allow us to approach the left-leaning hernia from the right side.
The special equipment we used for this case included a DaVinci XI robot, ProGrass forceps, monopolar curved scissors, and a mega needle driver.

The surgeon enters the retro-rectus space using a ten millimeter OptiView trocar, performing blunt dissection with the trocar and insufflation. A ligature device is then used for further dissection. Two additional eight millimeter ports are placed, and the DaVinci XI robot is docked. The right rectus space is large, allowing the surgeon to work medially across to the linea alba along the length of the rectus muscle.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath superior to the hernia to enter the preperitoneal space above the false form ligament, crossing over to the opposite side. The incision is repeated on the patient's left side, extending across the arcuate line and into the preperitoneal space below the hernia, isolating the hernia sac, which is then opened.

The surgeon circumferentially dissects the hernia sac, revealing it to be larger than previously noted, approximately six centimeters. The dissection extends into the left retro-rectus space, further developing this area. After complete dissection of the hernia sac, the posterior rectus sheath is reassessed, showing sufficient laxity for tension-free approximation.

The surgeon closes the diastasis and ventral hernia from superior to inferior using a running suture technique with a number one Stratifix. The hernia sac is imbricated by taking bites, ensuring no skin is included. Multiple throws are made in a row and tightened sequentially after reducing abdominal pressure to minimize tension on each stitch.
The surgeon uses a two L V-lock suture to re-approximate the edges of the posterior rectus fascia in a running fashion from inferior to superior, ensuring closure without undue tension.

The surgeon measures the retrorectus space to be twenty-six by sixteen centimeters and cuts a monofilament polypropylene macroporous mesh into an ovoid shape. The mesh is placed within the retrorectus space, fitting well without the need for fixation. The retrorectus space is surveyed for hemostasis, and a nineteen French Blake drain is left in place after desufflation.

The patient was discharged on post-op day one. Unfortunately, later the next day, he developed nausea and bilious emesis and presented to the emergency room, where a CT scan revealed a small bowel obstruction. The transition point was in the anterior abdomen, adjacent to where we would expect the posterior fascial closure to be.

During diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon uses three trocar sites to inspect the abdominal cavity. The posterior fascial closure is visibly disrupted, and a loop of small bowel is adherent to the mesh. The surgeon carefully reduces the small bowel, which appears viable.
The surgeon observes a large defect area, likely due to abdominal distension, and proceeds to close the fascial defect using a composite macroporous mesh placed in an intraperitoneal onlay fashion.
After this operation, the patient did well and was discharged home post-op day three after return of bowel function.
